faqs.q: What is the difference between a phobia and ordinary fear?
faqs.a: A specific phobia isn't ordinary dislike or mild nervousness. The clinical line is crossed when the fear is intense, persistent, usually lasting six months or more, and starts limiting what a person can do.

faqs.q: Why does avoiding a phobia make it worse?
faqs.a: The relief that comes from avoiding the trigger feels good in the moment, but it tends to keep the fear strong over time. That's part of why structured treatment tends to work better than willpower alone.
Yes. Specific phobias are among the most treatable anxiety conditions, and exposure therapy is the most studied treatment. Many people improve substantially with focused care.
